title: Mental Health — Substance Use & Withdrawal (Set 5)
subtitle: Alcohol/CIWA/DTs, opioids/naloxone/buprenorphine, stimulants, disulfiram & MAT
scenario: Caring for clients experiencing substance intoxication and withdrawal, and supporting medication-assisted treatment and recovery.

steps.q: A client's last alcoholic drink was about 8 hours ago. Which early withdrawal findings would the technician expect?

opts.text: Anxiety, tremor, insomnia, and mild autonomic arousal
opts.rat: Early alcohol withdrawal (about 6–12 hours after the last drink) produces anxiety, tremor, insomnia, sweating, and mild tachycardia.
These are signs of opioid intoxication, not alcohol withdrawal.
These are not features of alcohol withdrawal.

opts.text: No symptoms for at least a week
opts.rat: Withdrawal typically begins within hours, not after a week.

steps.q: About 48–72 hours after his last drink, a client becomes disoriented, febrile, tremulous, and reports seeing bugs on the wall. This is MOST consistent with:

opts.rat: Opioid withdrawal does not typically cause this level of delirium and fever.
Delirium tremens typically peaks 48–72 hours after the last drink, with autonomic hyperactivity, disorientation, fever, tremor, and hallucinations; it is a medical emergency.
A panic attack does not cause fever and disorientation over days.
This is a medical emergency, not normal.

steps.q: The CIWA-Ar scale is used during alcohol withdrawal mainly to:

opts.rat: The CIWA-Ar assesses symptoms, not blood alcohol level.
It does not screen for depression.
The CIWA-Ar quantifies withdrawal severity so benzodiazepines can be dosed on a symptom-triggered basis.
It is unrelated to diet planning.

steps.q: The primary medication class used to manage alcohol withdrawal is:

opts.rat: Opioids are not used to treat alcohol withdrawal.
Stimulants would worsen withdrawal.
Antipsychotics are not the primary treatment for alcohol withdrawal.
Benzodiazepines are first-line for alcohol withdrawal, reducing seizures, agitation, and progression to delirium tremens through cross-tolerance.

steps.q: A client in alcohol withdrawal is ordered thiamine before any IV glucose. Giving thiamine first helps prevent:

opts.rat: Giving glucose to a thiamine-deficient client can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy; thiamine is given first to prevent it.
Thiamine is not given to prevent hypoglycemia.
This is not the purpose of thiamine here.
Thiamine is unrelated to constipation.

steps.q: Wernicke encephalopathy from thiamine deficiency classically presents with:

opts.rat: These are not features of Wernicke encephalopathy.
The classic Wernicke triad is confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia/nystagmus, related to thiamine deficiency.
These suggest opioid effect, not Wernicke.
Rash is not part of the Wernicke triad.

steps.q: A client with chronic alcohol use is prescribed thiamine and folic acid mainly to:

opts.rat: These vitamins do not reverse intoxication.
Benzodiazepines, not vitamins, prevent withdrawal seizures.

opts.text: Correct common nutritional deficiencies of chronic alcohol use
opts.rat: Chronic alcohol use causes thiamine and folate deficiency; supplementation prevents complications such as Wernicke encephalopathy and anemia.
This is not the purpose of the supplementation.

steps.q: A client is found unresponsive with slow, shallow breathing and pinpoint pupils after suspected opioid use. The technician anticipates administration of:

opts.rat: A benzodiazepine would further depress respiration.
More opioid would worsen the overdose.
This does not treat opioid overdose.
Naloxone is an opioid antagonist used to reverse opioid overdose and its respiratory depression.

steps.q: Because naloxone has a shorter duration of action than many opioids, the technician should:

opts.text: Continue to monitor for return of respiratory depression, as repeat doses may be needed
opts.rat: Because naloxone wears off before some opioids, the client must be monitored for recurring respiratory depression and may need repeat dosing.

opts.text: Assume one dose is always sufficient and stop monitoring
opts.rat: Opioid effects can outlast naloxone, so continued monitoring is essential.

opts.text: Send the client home immediately after one dose
opts.rat: Early discharge is dangerous due to possible re-sedation.
Ongoing observation and care are required.

steps.q: Which findings are typical of OPIOID WITHDRAWAL (rather than intoxication)?

opts.rat: These are signs of opioid intoxication, not withdrawal.

opts.text: Dilated pupils, yawning, muscle aches, and diarrhea
opts.rat: Opioid withdrawal produces dilated pupils, yawning, lacrimation, rhinorrhea, muscle aches, and GI upset—uncomfortable but usually not life-threatening.
These are effects of opioid use, not withdrawal.
These are opioid intoxication effects.

steps.q: To avoid precipitated withdrawal, buprenorphine for opioid use disorder should be started when the client is:

opts.text: Has just used a full-agonist opioid
opts.rat: Starting too soon after opioid use precipitates acute withdrawal.

opts.text: Completely free of any withdrawal symptoms
opts.rat: Induction is timed to moderate withdrawal, not the absence of symptoms.

opts.text: In moderate withdrawal (e.g., an elevated COWS score)
opts.rat: Buprenorphine is a partial agonist that can displace full agonists; starting it in moderate withdrawal prevents precipitated withdrawal.
Induction is not done during sedation/intoxication.

steps.q: Which medications are approved for medication-assisted treatment (MAT) of opioid use disorder?

opts.rat: Disulfiram is for alcohol use disorder; thiamine is a vitamin, not MAT.
These are mood stabilizers, not opioid MAT.
These are used in psychosis/EPS, not for opioid use disorder.
Methadone, buprenorphine, and naltrexone are the approved medications for opioid use disorder treatment.

steps.q: A client taking disulfiram must be taught to avoid:

opts.text: Any product containing alcohol, including mouthwash and some cough syrups
opts.rat: Disulfiram causes a severe reaction with any alcohol, including hidden sources in mouthwash, cough syrup, and aftershave.
Tyramine restriction applies to MAOIs, not disulfiram.
These are not restricted with disulfiram.
Water is not restricted.

steps.q: A client who used methamphetamine is agitated, paranoid, and has a rapid heart rate. An appropriate technician intervention is to:

opts.text: Place the client in a bright, noisy dayroom
opts.rat: Overstimulation worsens agitation and paranoia.

opts.text: Provide a calm, low-stimulation environment and ensure safety while monitoring
opts.rat: Stimulant intoxication causes agitation and paranoia; reducing stimulation, ensuring safety, and monitoring vital signs are appropriate.
Exercise increases cardiac demand in a client who is already tachycardic.
An agitated, paranoid client needs monitoring for safety.

steps.q: A client withdrawing from long-term stimulant (cocaine/methamphetamine) use is most at risk for:

opts.rat: Seizures are characteristic of alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal, not typical stimulant withdrawal.
Withdrawal produces the opposite—fatigue and low mood.

opts.text: Depression, fatigue, and suicidal ideation during the 'crash'
opts.rat: Stimulant withdrawal produces a 'crash' with intense fatigue, hypersomnia, depression, and increased suicide risk requiring monitoring.
Pinpoint pupils relate to opioids, not stimulant withdrawal.

steps.q: The MOST therapeutic attitude when caring for a client with a substance use disorder is to be:

opts.text: Judgmental and shaming to motivate change
opts.rat: Shaming increases stigma and undermines engagement in treatment.
Dismissiveness damages the therapeutic relationship.
A punitive stance discourages honesty and help-seeking.

opts.text: Nonjudgmental, respectful, and supportive of the client's treatment
opts.rat: A nonjudgmental, respectful, stigma-free approach supports engagement and recovery.

steps.q: Alcohol-withdrawal seizures most commonly occur within which time frame after the last drink?

opts.rat: Alcohol-withdrawal seizures most commonly occur roughly 6–48 hours after the last drink; delirium tremens typically follows.
Seizures do not usually occur within the first hour.
This is far too late for withdrawal seizures.
This is not the withdrawal-seizure window.

steps.q: A client recovering from opioid use disorder is being discharged. Part of harm-reduction teaching includes:

opts.text: Telling the client relapse means treatment has failed permanently
opts.rat: Framing relapse as permanent failure discourages continued treatment; relapse can be part of the recovery process.

opts.text: Providing take-home naloxone and teaching its use
opts.rat: Take-home naloxone and overdose education are key harm-reduction measures that can save lives if relapse occurs.

opts.text: Advising the client to hide their treatment from all providers
opts.rat: Concealing treatment can lead to unsafe care.

opts.text: Recommending the client stop all follow-up care
opts.rat: Ongoing follow-up supports recovery.

steps.q: Which sign indicates OPIOID intoxication rather than withdrawal?

opts.rat: These are withdrawal signs, not intoxication.
These are withdrawal signs.
Opioid intoxication causes miosis (pinpoint pupils), sedation, and respiratory depression.
These occur in withdrawal, not intoxication.

steps.q: During alcohol withdrawal, the technician should monitor and report vital-sign changes because delirium tremens is associated with:

opts.rat: DTs produce autonomic hyperactivity, not primarily bradycardia and hypothermia.
Vital signs are typically markedly abnormal in DTs.
The concern is autonomic instability, not appetite.

opts.text: Autonomic instability such as fever, tachycardia, and hypertension
opts.rat: Delirium tremens causes dangerous autonomic hyperactivity (fever, tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis) that must be monitored and reported.

reference: Aligned to the California BVNPT Psychiatric Technician exam content area: Mental Health Care. Written from standard psychiatric-technician and nursing references. Pending faculty review.
